lectureTitle: JSON Web Tokens and Verifiable Credentials (+ PwC Guest)

examQuestions.promptVerbatim: How is a JWT (JSON Web Token) different from a session ID? In what scenarios would you prefer using JWT over traditional session management?
Explain the structure of a JWT (JSON Web Token). What does the header typically contain? What types of claims can be found in the payload? How is the signature generated and what role does it play?
Describe how Verifiable Credentials (VCs) work. What are the components of a VC (Verifiable Credential)? Who are the key roles (issuer, holder, verifier), and what do they do?

drills.question: A teammate says 'a JWT (JSON Web Token) is just a fancy session ID.' Where does the server store each, and why does that distinction matter?
keyBeats: session ID = random string, server stores state
JWT (JSON Web Token) = self-contained, server keeps no session state
session ID is a lookup key; JWT (JSON Web Token) carries the claims itself
drills.modelAnswer: A session ID is a randomly generated string that the server stores server-side (DB (Database) or in-memory cache) along with the user's data; the ID is just a lookup key. A JWT (JSON Web Token) is stateless and self-contained: the server creates it after authentication and does not store it server-side, so all the claims (userID, role, permissions) travel inside the token itself and are stored only client-side.

drills.question: Your app runs behind a load balancer across several replica servers and sessions keep breaking on different servers. Why, and how do JWTs (JSON Web Tokens) help?
keyBeats: stateful sessions tied to one server
workarounds: shared cache or sticky sessions
JWT (JSON Web Token) is stateless -> any server can validate -> scales
drills.modelAnswer: With stateful sessions the session data lives on the server that created it, so a request hitting a different replica fails; the deck shows fixes like a shared Redis cache or sticky sessions, both with drawbacks. Because a JWT (JSON Web Token) is stateless and self-contained, any server can verify it without a shared session store, which simplifies scalability and efficient request handling.

drills.question: Name two concrete scenarios where you'd prefer a JWT (JSON Web Token) over traditional session management.
keyBeats: authorization after login (most common)
SSO (Single Sign-On): low overhead + cross-domain
OIDC (OpenID Connect) ID Token
information exchange between parties
drills.modelAnswer: Authorization is the most common case: after login each request carries the JWT (JSON Web Token) so the user can reach resources allowed by its claims. JWTs suit Single Sign On because of low overhead and cross-domain usage, and the OIDC (OpenID Connect) ID Token is a JWT. They are also good for secure information exchange between parties since being signed lets the recipient verify integrity.

drills.question: The statelessness that makes JWTs (JSON Web Tokens) scalable creates one operational headache. What is it, and what's a partial fix?
keyBeats: hard to revoke before expiry
server-side blocklist contradicts statelessness
refresh tokens get new tokens without re-auth
drills.modelAnswer: Because JWTs (JSON Web Tokens) are stateless, it is hard to revoke one before its expiration time. A possible fix is a server-side blocklist, but that contradicts the no-state idea. Refresh tokens are a related artifact that let a client obtain new access tokens without forcing the user to authenticate again.

drills.question: Where exactly is a JWT (JSON Web Token) stored, and where is it explicitly NOT stored?
keyBeats: NOT stored server-side
stored client-side only
examples: local storage, HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) cookies, in-memory, IndexedDB
drills.modelAnswer: A JWT (JSON Web Token) is created by the server after authentication but is not stored at the server side. It is stored only at the client side, for example in local storage, HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) cookies, in-memory, or IndexedDB. This is the inverse of a session ID, whose authoritative state lives on the server.

drills.question: Sketch the three-part structure of a JWT (JSON Web Token) as it appears on the wire and how the parts are joined.
keyBeats: header.payload.signature
three base64url parts
separated by dots ('.')
drills.modelAnswer: A JWT (JSON Web Token) is three base64url-encoded strings separated by dots: header.payload.signature (aaaaaa.bbbbbb.cccccc). The format is URL (Uniform Resource Locator)-safe so it can be passed easily in HTML (HyperText Markup Language) and HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) environments. The RFC (Request for Comments) describes it as a sequence of URL-safe parts separated by period characters.

drills.question: What two things does a JWT (JSON Web Token) header (JOSE (JSON Object Signing and Encryption) header) typically contain, and what does each mean?
keyBeats: alg = signing algorithm (e.g. HS256)
typ = token type, JWT (JSON Web Token)
header describes the crypto operations
drills.modelAnswer: The header typically has two parts: 'typ', the type of the token (JWT (JSON Web Token)), and 'alg', the signing algorithm the server used, e.g. HMAC (Hash-based Message Authentication Code) SHA256. Per the RFC (Request for Comments) the JOSE (JSON Object Signing and Encryption) Header describes the cryptographic operations applied to the JWT, and 'typ' (recommended value 'JWT') disambiguates the object's media type.

drills.question: The payload holds claims. What are the three classes of claims, and how do they differ?
keyBeats: registered: predefined, optional but recommended
public: defined at will, collision-resistant
private: agreed between producer/consumer, collision-prone
drills.modelAnswer: Claims are statements about an entity. The RFC (Request for Comments) defines three classes: Registered claim names (predefined, optional but recommended, e.g. iss); Public claim names (defined at will but should be collision-resistant or IANA (Internet Assigned Numbers Authority)-registered to avoid collisions); and Private claim names (custom names a producer and consumer agree to use, which are subject to collision and used with caution).

drills.question: List the seven registered claim names from RFC 7519 and give a one-line meaning for each.
keyBeats: iss issuer, sub subject, aud audience
exp expiration, nbf not-before, iat issued-at
jti unique JWT (JSON Web Token) ID (anti-replay)
drills.modelAnswer: iss identifies the principal that issued the JWT (JSON Web Token); sub identifies the subject the claims are about; aud identifies the intended recipients; exp is the expiration time on/after which the JWT must not be accepted; nbf is the time before which it must not be accepted; iat is when the JWT was issued; and jti is a unique identifier that can be used to prevent replay.

drills.question: How is the JWT (JSON Web Token) signature computed, and what two inputs besides the secret/key feed into it?
keyBeats: sign over base64url(header) + '.' + base64url(payload)
plus a secret/key and the chosen algorithm
e.g. HMAC (Hash-based Message Authentication Code)-SHA256(header.payload, secret)
drills.modelAnswer: The signature is computed over the encoded header plus the encoded payload, using a secret (or key) and the chosen algorithm. For an HS256 token it is HMAC (Hash-based Message Authentication Code)-SHA256(base64urlEncoding(header) + '.' + base64urlEncoding(payload), secret). With an asymmetric algorithm like RS256/ES256 the issuer signs with its private key instead.

drills.question: What role does the signature play, and what extra guarantee do you get if it was made with a private key?
keyBeats: integrity: message not changed in transit
private key also proves the sender
verifiable via JWS (JSON Web Signature); HMAC (Hash-based Message Authentication Code) vs RSA (Rivest-Shamir-Adleman)/ECDSA (Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm)
drills.modelAnswer: The signature lets the recipient verify the message was not changed along the way; if it was signed with a private key it also verifies the sender of the JWT (JSON Web Token). JWTs are digitally signed via JWS (JSON Web Signature) and can be signed with a secret (HMAC (Hash-based Message Authentication Code)) or a public/private key pair (RSA (Rivest-Shamir-Adleman) or ECDSA (Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm)), which is what makes the token verifiable and trustworthy.

drills.question: JWS (JSON Web Signature) and JWE (JSON Web Encryption) both appear around JWTs (JSON Web Tokens). What does each provide, and which is the focus for tokens?
keyBeats: JWS (JSON Web Signature) = signing/integrity (MAC (Message Authentication Code) or signature)
JWE (JSON Web Encryption) = encryption/confidentiality
focus is on signed tokens
drills.modelAnswer: JWS (JSON Web Signature) provides the cryptographic signature for authenticity and integrity, while JWE (JSON Web Encryption) provides confidentiality by encrypting the content. JWTs (JSON Web Tokens) can be encrypted, but the focus is on signed tokens; encryption is used when you also need to hide the information.

drills.question: An attacker submits a token with header alg=none. Per the JWT (JSON Web Token) attacks slide, what is the attack and the mitigation?
keyBeats: alg=none -> server skips validation
goal: bypass auth / impersonate
mitigation: reject alg=none, whitelist algorithms
drills.modelAnswer: Setting 'alg' to 'none' instructs the server to skip token validation, letting an attacker forge a token and bypass authentication or impersonate an authenticated user. The mitigation is to reject JWTs (JSON Web Tokens) with alg=none and only accept tokens signed using algorithms specified in a whitelist. Related attacks are payload tampering and secret brute-forcing.

drills.question: What is a Verifiable Credential (VC), and what are its three components?
keyBeats: tamper-evident claims + metadata, cryptographically provable issuer
components: credential metadata, claims, proof
proof = issuer's digital signature
drills.modelAnswer: A VC (Verifiable Credential) is a set of tamper-evident claims and metadata that cryptographically prove who issued it. Its three components are: Credential Metadata (info about the credential such as issuer, type, dates), Claims (the actual information, e.g. degree or name), and Proof (a cryptographic signature ensuring authenticity, provided by the issuer).

drills.question: Use the diploma analogy from the deck to explain the three VC (Verifiable Credential) components to a non-technical person.
keyBeats: metadata = diploma header (university, issue date)
claims = student name, degree, GPA (Grade Point Average)
proof = official stamp & signature
drills.modelAnswer: Think of a diploma. The Credential Metadata is the header on the diploma (university name, issue date). The Claims are the actual content, the student's name, degree and GPA (Grade Point Average). The Proof is the official university stamp and signature, which in a VC (Verifiable Credential) is a cryptographic signature ensuring authenticity.

drills.question: Name the three key roles in a VC (Verifiable Credential) ecosystem and state what each one does.
keyBeats: issuer issues the VC (Verifiable Credential) (university, government, employer)
holder stores it in a digital wallet (student, citizen)
verifier checks authenticity (employer, airport, website)
drills.modelAnswer: The three players are the Issuer (a university, government or employer that issues the verifiable credential), the Holder (a student, employee or citizen who stores the credential in a digital wallet), and the Verifier (an employer, airport security or website that verifies the credential's authenticity).

drills.question: Describe the VC (Verifiable Credential) 'trust triangle': how do the three roles relate and where does trust actually come from?
keyBeats: issuer -> holder: issues credential
holder -> verifier: presents credential
verifier trusts issuer's signature, not the holder
drills.modelAnswer: The issuer issues a credential to the holder, who stores it in a wallet; the holder presents it to a verifier when needed. The verifier does not have to contact the issuer at verification time, it checks the issuer's cryptographic proof against the issuer's public key. So trust flows from the verifier's trust in the issuer, with the holder in the middle controlling presentation.

drills.question: A VC (Verifiable Credential) can be JWT (JSON Web Token)-encoded or JSON-LD-encoded. How does the proof differ between the two?
keyBeats: JWT (JSON Web Token)/JWS (JSON Web Signature): credential mapped into JWT payload
JWS (JSON Web Signature) signature is an external proof
JSON-LD: Linked Data Proofs embedded in the object
drills.modelAnswer: A JSON-encoded VC (Verifiable Credential) is based on JSON Web Tokens (JWT/JWS (JSON Web Signature)): the credential is mapped into a JWT payload and the JWT signature acts as an external proof. A JSON-LD-encoded VC is based on Linked Data Proofs, where proofs are embedded into various parts of the credential object. JSON-LD also adds semantic disambiguation via linked-data context.

drills.question: In a JWT (JSON Web Token)-encoded VC (Verifiable Credential) (JWT-VC), which claims sit in the payload alongside the 'vc' object?
keyBeats: header: alg, typ, kid
payload: iss, nbf, exp, jti, sub + vc
signature = external proof
drills.modelAnswer: A JWT-VC has three sections. The header contains alg, typ and kid (which public key to validate with). The payload contains iss, nbf, exp, jti and sub claims plus the vc credential object (@context, type, credentialSubject). The signature is the signature of the encoded header and payload, serving as the external proof.

drills.question: What is JSON-LD and what problem does it solve that plain JSON does not?
keyBeats: extends JSON with RDF (Resource Description Framework)/Linked Data
@context gives terms machine-meaning
computers understand relationships, not just labels
drills.modelAnswer: JSON-LD extends traditional JSON by incorporating features from RDF (Resource Description Framework); it is a human-readable serialization for linked data. Plain JSON labels like 'name' and 'school' are just strings to a computer, but with an @context and @type a machine can understand that John Doe is a Person who attended a School, capturing the relationships.

drills.question: (PwC guest) The IGA (Identity Governance and Administration) deck frames the whole discipline around one question. What is it, and what is IGA broadly about?
keyBeats: 'who has access to what - and why?'
managing users and access rights in connected systems
improving IT (Information Technology) security and compliance
drills.modelAnswer: IGA (Identity Governance and Administration) answers the question: do you know who has access to what, and why? It is about managing users and access rights across connected applications and IT (Information Technology) systems, improving overall IT security by supporting key IT controls, and staying compliant with regulations and good practices. PAM (Privileged Access Management), by contrast, secures privileged access.

drills.question: (PwC guest) RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) vs ABAC (Attribute-Based Access Control): which is admin-time vs runtime, and what PIP (Policy Information Point)/PDP (Policy Decision Point)/PEP (Policy Enforcement Point) roles appear?
keyBeats: RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) = admin-time preauthorize (provision entitlements)
ABAC (Attribute-Based Access Control) = runtime real-time decision on attributes
PIP (Policy Information Point) info, PDP (Policy Decision Point) decision, PEP (Policy Enforcement Point) enforcement
drills.modelAnswer: RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) is access administration done at admin time: roles and entitlements are preauthorized and provisioned. ABAC (Attribute-Based Access Control) is access enforcement at runtime: a real-time decision is made from user/resource attributes. The policy components are PIP (Policy Information Point), PDP (Policy Decision Point) and PEP (Policy Enforcement Point), and authorization logic can be embedded in the app or externalized.

drills.question: How does the holder actually present a Verifiable Credential (VC) to a verifier, and what stops it being replayed?
keyBeats: Holder presents the VC (Verifiable Credential) as a Verifiable Presentation (VP)
The presentation is signed by the HOLDER (not just the issuer)
It includes a nonce supplied by the verifier, so it can't be replayed
Holder presents the whole credential even if only some attributes are needed (selective disclosure is a separate mechanism)
drills.modelAnswer: The holder presents a credential to a verifier as a VP (Verifiable Presentation). The presentation is signed by the holder with a nonce supplied by the verifier, so a captured presentation cannot be replayed to a different verifier or at a later time. In the basic model the holder presents the entire credential as a verifiable presentation even if some attributes are not needed for the check — completing the issuer -> holder -> verifier trust triangle on the holder side.
drills.citation: IAM - JWT.txt, lines 1148-1151: 'Signed by the holder with a nonce from the verifier so that it can't be replayed... present the entire credential as a verifiable presentation'

exercises.promptVerbatim: Go to https://jwt.io/ ... Try to change the payload, and the secret ... Go to https://token.dev/ and paste the JWT (JSON Web Token) ... paste the secret you created. The signature is now "verified"
exercises.whatItTests: Hands-on understanding that header/payload are only base64url-encoded (readable) while the signature requires the correct signing key to verify.
exercises.answerSketch: Decoding shows header and payload in clear; verification fails until the matching secret is supplied, after which the signature verifies. Demonstrates integrity protection, not confidentiality.

exercises.promptVerbatim: How do JWTs (JSON Web Tokens) differ from Certificates ?
exercises.whatItTests: Conceptual contrast between a JWT (JSON Web Token) (compact, self-contained signed claims set, typically short-lived) and an X.509 certificate (binds a public key to an identity, signed by a CA (Certificate Authority), used in PKI (Public Key Infrastructure)).
exercises.answerSketch: Both are signed and verifiable, but a JWT (JSON Web Token) carries arbitrary claims for transmitting authz (authorization)/identity data between parties and is usually short-lived; a certificate binds a public key to an identity via a CA (Certificate Authority) chain for establishing trust/TLS (Transport Layer Security). Not stated verbatim in slides.

exercises.promptVerbatim: For all these changes, the IGA (Identity Governance and Administration) solution must re-calculate the rules-based access assignments. How many of these Organizational changes can be automatically handled by the IGA solution? Which changes requires some sort of manual input?
exercises.whatItTests: Whether organizational-data-driven (rules-based) access can be auto-recalculated on org changes, and recognizing which changes need human judgment.
exercises.answerSketch: Changes cleanly expressible in org data (new/closed/moved department, new manager) can be auto-recalculated; ambiguous ones (split/merge, employees reassigned) typically need manual input to map people to new units/roles.
